title: Comparing data distributions
description: Take a look at two different data distributions and draw some comparisons.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kenny interviewed freshmen and seniors at his high school, asking them how many pieces of fruit they eat each day.  The results are shown in the $2$ plots below.

**The mean number of pieces of fruit is greater for [[☃ dropdown 1]].**
                        
**The mean is a good measure of the center of the distribution for [[☃ dropdown 3]].**

$\quad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\text{Freshmen}$  


[[☃ image 1]]

                                 
$\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\text{Seniors}$  


[[☃ image 2]]
hints:
The mean number of fruits eaten by freshmen is  

$\dfrac{(1\times0)+(2\times1)+(2\times 2)+(4\times3)+(3\times4)+(1\times5)+(1\times6) +(1\times19)}{15}=4 \text{ fruits}$

The mean number of fruits eaten by seniors is  

$\dfrac{(1\times0)+(1\times1)+(2\times 2)+(5\times3)+(3\times4)+(2\times5)+(1\times6) +(1\times7)}{16}\approx 3.4 \text{ fruits}$

The mean for freshmen is greater.

The distribution of fruits eaten by freshmen has one very large outlier.  Large outliers can strongly affect the mean of a distribution, making it an inappropriate measure of the center.  The mean is a better measure of the center for seniors.

**The correct answers are:**

- **The mean number of fruits is greater for freshmen.**             
- **The mean is a good measure of the center of the distribution for seniors.**

options.alt: A dot plot where there is a cluster of dots ranging from 0 to 6 with a peak at 3. There is a single dot at 19.
A dot plot where there is a cluster of dots ranging from 0 to 7 with a peak at 3.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tara is considering choosing either anthropology or economics as her college major. She's wondering how her SAT scores compare to those of her peers in either major. Tara finds some data on the SAT scores of anthropology and economics majors at her school. The data is shown in the $2$ plots below.  

**For [[☃ dropdown 1]] majors, the standard deviation is an appropriate measure of spread.**
                        
**The [[☃ dropdown 2]] majors have a greater standard deviation in SAT scores.**

$\quad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\text{Anthropology}$  


[[☃ image 1]]

                                 
$\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\text{Economics}$  


[[☃ image 2]]
hints: The distribution of SAT scores for anthropology majors is approximately normal.  For normal distributions, the standard deviation is a good measure of spread.  For flat distributions, like the scores of economics majors, the standard deviation is not a good measure of spread.
The standard deviation measures how spread out the data are from the mean.  The data for economics majors are much more spread out than for anthropology majors.

**The correct answers are:**

- **For anthropology majors, the standard deviation is an appropriate measure of spread.**                        
- **The economics majors have a greater standard deviation in SAT scores.**

options.alt: A dot plot, where there is the following distribution: 1775, 1; 1825, 2; 1850, 4; 1875, 5; 1900, 7; 1925, 6; 1950, 4; 1975, 2; 2000, 1.
A dot plot, where there is the following distribution: 1700, 3; 1750, 2; 1800, 3; 1850, 3; 1900, 3; 1950, 2; 2000, 3; 2050, 3; 2100, 3.

assessment_items.question_content:
Maddie wanted to see how lung capacity varies among endurance athletes at her high school.     She sampled athletes on the crew and cross country teams and recorded how many seconds each athlete was able to hold his or her breath.  Her data is shown in the $2$ plots below.  

**The [[☃ dropdown 1]] team has a greater median.**
                        
**The [[☃ dropdown 2]] team has a greater standard deviation.**

$\quad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\text{Cross country}$  
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d380acf6930875d7d6716097adf3cbbe.png)
                                 
$\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\text{Crew}$  
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/211f7a48ad646438fd283586a7e6d027.png)
hints:
In a box plot, the median is located at the middle vertical bar:  

[[☃ image 1]]

  

The median of the cross country team is about $75$ seconds.

In a dot plot, the median is found by locating the middle data point.  There are $23$ members of the crew team, and the $12$th highest lung capacity is about $93$ seconds.  The median for crew is higher.
The standard deviation measures how spread out the data are from the mean.  All the members of the crew team fall in a $30$-second range\.  How spread out are the members of the cross country team?

The plot below highlights the distance between the left vertical bar and the right vertical bar on the box plot. This distance shows the range for the middle $50\%$ of the data set. Only half of the cross country members fall in this $65$-second range.  Therefore, the cross country data are much more spread out than the crew data.  
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/dcd4d6fd931f6f9a6d8ccafa3f4a1d54.png)

**The correct answers are:**

- **The crew team has a greater median.**
- **The cross country team has a greater standard deviation.**

assessment_items.question_content:
Kenny interviewed freshmen and seniors at his high school, asking them how many pieces of fruit they eat each day.  The results are shown in the $2$ plots below.

**The standard deviation is greater for [[☃ dropdown 1]].**
                        
**The standard deviation is a good measure of the spread of the distribution for [[☃ dropdown 2]].**

$\quad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\text{Freshmen}$  


[[☃ image 1]]

                                 
$\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\text{Seniors}$  


[[☃ image 2]]
hints: The standard deviation measures how spread out the data are from the mean.  The mean number of pieces of fruit eaten by freshmen is some value between $0$ and $6$. But there are several data points on each extreme.  Most of the data are not clustered around the mean.
The data for seniors resembles a normal distribution.  Most of the data points are clustered around the center.  The standard deviation for freshmen is greater.
In a normal distribution, the standard deviation is an appropriate measure of the spread.  The data set for seniors more closely resembles a normal distribution.

**The correct answers are:**

- **The standard deviation is greater for freshmen.**
- **The standard deviation is a good measure of the spread of the distribution for seniors.**

options.alt: A dot plot, where there is the following distribution: 0, 4; 1, 2; 2, 1; 3, 2; 4, 3; 5, 4.
A dot plot, where there is the following distribution: 0, 1; 1, 1; 2, 2; 3, 4; 4, 3; 5, 1.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ben is concerned that he is not reading enough.  He decides to record how many pages he reads each day for the month of March, and he makes a box plot of the data.  Then he repeats the process for the month of April.  His $2$ plots are shown below.

**The interquartile range for pages read is greater for  [[☃ dropdown 1]].**
                        
**The median number of pages read is greater for [[☃ dropdown 2]].**

$\quad\qquad\qquad\quad\qquad\qquad\quad\qquad\text{March}$  


[[☃ image 1]]

                                 
$\qquad\qquad\qquad\quad\qquad\qquad\quad\qquad\text{April}$  

[[☃ image 2]]
hints:
In a box plot, the median is given by the middle vertical line.  The median for March is $20$ pages.  


[[☃ image 1]]

The median for April is $25$ pages.


[[☃ image 1]]

In a box plot, the interquartile range (IQR) is the distance between the left vertical bar and the right vertical bar:  


[[☃ image 1]]

 
The IQR for March is $10$ pages.  
The IQR for April is $20$ pages.


[[☃ image 1]]

**The correct answers are:**

- **The interquartile range for pages read is greater for April.**
- **The median number of pages read is greater for April.**

options.alt: A box and whisker plot that ranges from 5 to 60. The box ranges from 15 to 25. The vertical line dividing the box is at 20.
A box and whisker plot that ranges from 10 to 40. The box ranges from 15 to 35. The vertical line dividing the box is at 25.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tara is considering choosing either anthropology or economics as her college major, and she's wondering how her SAT scores compare to those of her peers in either major. Tara finds some data on the SAT scores of anthropology and economics majors at her school. The data is shown in the $2$ plots below.  

**For [[☃ dropdown 1]] majors, the mean SAT score is greater than the median.**
                        
**The [[☃ dropdown 2]] majors have a greater median SAT score.**

$\quad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\text{Anthropology}$  


[[☃ image 1]]

                                 
$\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\text{Economics}$  


[[☃ image 2]]
hints:
The distribution of SAT scores for anthropology majors is skewed right.  In a skewed right distribution, the additional high values on the right side of the plot cause the mean to be greater than the median.  

The distribution of SAT scores for economics majors is about symmetrical, so the median will be very close to the mean.
To find the median value in a dot plot, we simply locate the middle data point.  There are $35$ anthropology majors, and the $18$th highest score is $1850$.
There were $29$ economics majors, and the $15$th highest score is $1900$.  The median for economics majors is greater.

**The correct answers are:**

- **For anthropology majors, the mean SAT score is greater than the median.**
- **The economics majors have a greater median SAT score.**

options.alt: A dot plot has a horizontal axis marked from 1400 to 2400 in increments of 50. The number of dots above each value is as follows. 1650, 1; 1700, 2; 1750, 4; 1800, 6; 1850, 6; 1900, 5; 1950, 4; 2000, 2; 2050, 2; 2100, 1; 2150, 1; 2200, 1. The values not mentioned do not have dots above them. All values are estimated.

A dot plot has a horizontal axis marked from 1400 to 2400 in increments of 50. The number of dots above each value is as follows. 1750, 2; 1800, 4; 1850, 5; 1900, 8; 1950, 5; 2000, 4; 2050, 1. The values not mentioned do not have dots above them. All values are estimated.

assessment_items.question_content:
Maddie wanted to see how lung capacity varies among endurance athletes at her high school.     She sampled athletes on the crew and cross country teams and recorded how many seconds each athlete was able to hold his or her breath.  Her data is shown in the $2$ plots below.  

**The [[☃ dropdown 1]] team has a greater difference between its mean and median.**
                        
**The [[☃ dropdown 2]] team has a greater interquartile range.**

$\quad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\text{Cross country}$  


[[☃ image 1]]

                                 
$\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\text{Crew}$  


[[☃ image 2]]
hints: In a symmetrical distribution the median and mean do not differ by much.  The crew team has a fairly symmetrical data set, but the cross country team does not $-$ it is skewed right.  We expect the cross country team to have a greater difference between its median and mean.

In a box plot, the interquartile range (IQR) is the distance between the left vertical bar and the right vertical bar:  


[[☃ image 1]]

The IQR of the cross country team is about $65$ seconds.

In a histogram, it’s difficult to precisely determine the IQR.  We can see, however, that the distance between the highest and lowest values on the crew team is only $30$, so the IQR must be less than $30$ seconds.

The correct answers are:

- The cross country team has a greater difference between its mean and median.  
- The cross country team has a greater interquartile range.

options.alt: A box and whisker plot where the box from the Q 1 to Q 3 ranges from 55 to 120 seconds. The line dividing the box is at 75.
A dot plot labeled seconds. All the dots are in a cluster ranging from 80 to 110. The peak of the cluster is at 95.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ben is concerned that he is not reading enough.  He decides to record how many pages he reads each day for the month of April, and he makes a box plot of the data.  Then he repeats the process for the month of June.  His $2$ plots are shown below.

**In  [[☃ dropdown 1]] there were $15$ days that Ben read between $25$ and $35$ pages.**
                        
**In  [[☃ dropdown 2]], Ben took a day off from reading.**

$\quad\qquad\qquad\quad\qquad\qquad\quad\qquad\text{April}$  


[[☃ image 1]]

                                 
$\qquad\qquad\qquad\quad\qquad\qquad\quad\qquad\text{June}$  


[[☃ image 2]]
hints: The interquartile range (IQR) is an interval that contains $50\%$ of the data points.  In this context, half of the data set is $15$ days, because we are examining months of $30$ days.

In a box plot, the interquartile range (IQR) is the distance between the left vertical bar and the right vertical bar:  


[[☃ image 1]]

In April, there were $15$ days that Ben read between $25$ and $35$ pages.

The left-most dot in a box plot shows the lowest value of the data set.  In April, the lowest value was $10$ pages, and in June the lowest value was $0$ pages.  There was a day in June where Ben took a day off from reading.

**The correct answers are:**

- **In April there were $15$ days that Ben read between $25$ and $35$ pages.**               
- **In June, Ben took a day off from reading.**

options.alt: A box and whisker plot that ranges from 10 to 90. The box ranges from 25 to 35. The vertical line dividing the box is at 30.
A box and whisker plot that ranges from 0 to 45. The box ranges from 15 to 35. The vertical line dividing the box is at 30.

assessment_items.question_content:
Caleb wants to rent an apartment, and he is concerned about paying for utilities.  When he visits apartments, he asks for data on monthly utility bills.  "University Heights Apartments" showed Caleb a box plot and "Park Center Apartments" showed Caleb a dot plot.  The $2$ plots are shown below; they give monthly utilities in dollars.

**[[☃ dropdown 1]] most likely has a greater difference between its median and its mean monthly utility bill.**
                        
**[[☃ dropdown 2]] has a greater interquartile range for monthly utilities.**

$\quad\qquad\qquad\text{Monthly utilities at University Heights Apartments}$  


[[☃ image 1]]

                                 
$\qquad\qquad\qquad\text{Monthly utility bills at Park Center Apartments}$  


[[☃ image 2]]
hints: In skewed distributions, the mean is likely to be different from the median.  The data for Park Center Apartments are skewed right, so the mean monthly utility bill should be greater than the median.

The box plot indicates a symmetric distribution of utility bills at University Heights Apartments.  We don't know the individual data points, but we expect that the mean utility bill is close to the median utility bill.

In a box plot, the interquartile range (IQR) is the distance between the left vertical bar and the right vertical bar.  


[[☃ image 1]]

The IQR of University Heights Apartments is about $\$20$.

The IQR is a range that contains the middle $50\%$ of the data set. There are about $70$ data points for Park Center Apartments, and the middle $35$ data points are contained in a range of about $\$35$.  The IQR for Park Center Apartments is greater.

**The correct answers are:**

- **Park Center Apartments most likely has a greater difference between its median and its mean monthly utility bill.**
- **Park Center Apartments has a greater interquartile range for monthly utilities.**

options.alt: A horizontal box plot is marked from 0 to 160 in increments of 10. A left whisker extends from 60 to 70. The box extends from 70 to 90 and is divided into 2 parts by a vertical line segment at 80. The right whisker extends from 90 to 100. All values estimated.

A dot plot has a horizontal axis marked from 0 to 160 in increments of 10. The number of dots above each value is as follows. 50, 1; 55, 2; 60, 6; 65, 8; 70, 7; 75, 7; 80, 5; 85, 5; 90, 4; 95, 4; 100, 4; 105, 3; 110, 3; 120, 3; 125, 2; 130, 2; 135, 1; 140, 1. The values not mentioned do not have dots above them.

assessment_items.question_content:
Caleb wants to rent an apartment, and he is concerned about paying for utilities.  When he visits apartments, he asks for data on monthly utility bills.  "University Heights Apartments" showed Caleb a box plot and "Park Center Apartments" showed Caleb a dot plot.  The $2$ plots are shown below; they give monthly utilities in dollars.

** [[☃ dropdown 1]] has a greater median monthly utility bill.**
                        
**[[☃ dropdown 2]] has a greater standard deviation for monthly utility bills.**

$\quad\qquad\qquad\text{Monthly utilities at University Heights Apartments}$  
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2e55f688733e019ecd2cb3b97d537b90.png)
                                 
$\qquad\qquad\qquad\text{Monthly utility bills at Park Center Apartments}$  


[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
In a box plot, the median is given by the middle vertical line.  The median for University Heights Apartments is $\$80$.  


[[☃ image 1]]
In a dot plot we can find the median by locating the middle value of the data set.  At Park Center Apartments, there are $8$ values of $\$60$ and $7$ values of $\$100$.  The median is $\$60$.

The standard deviation measures how far the data are spread out from the mean.  For University Heights Apartments, we know that $50\%$ of the data fall in a $\$20$ range, the interquartile range:![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1ddec9a41dd03f5f02eb50a1ac6d39e1.png)
For Park Center Apartments, the data fall into two extremes that are $\$40$ apart.  The data are further spread out for Park Center Apartments.

**The correct answers are:**

- ** University Heights Apartments has a greater median monthly utility bill.**
- **Park Center Apartments has a greater standard deviation for monthly utility bills.**

options.alt: A dot plot, where there is the following distribution: 60, 8; 100, 7.

assessment_items.question_content:
Doug is going to ride his bicycle $3{,}000$ miles across the United States, from coast-to-coast.  He wants to choose the route that will give him the greatest chance of success. Here's what he finds in his research:

-  There have been $2$ attempts on the northern route from Maine to Washington.  Both of those riders made it $2{,}000$ miles and quit in Montana.
- There have been $19$ attempts on the central route from Virginia to California;  $9$ of those riders didn't make it out of Virginia and the other $10$ made it all the way to the Pacific.
- There have been $32$ attempts on the southern route from Florida to San Diego.  One of those riders made it the whole way.  Another one went half a mile, realized he was out of shape, and quit. The other $30$ riders quit somewhere in Texas.  They were evenly distributed between $1{,}300$ and $1{,}700$ miles.


**Which of the following is true? **
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: There were only $2$ riders on the northern route, and they traveled the same distance, so there is absolutely no variability between these values.  The standard deviation for the northern route is $0$ miles, which is very low.

The IQR defines a range that contains the middle $50\%$ of a data set.  A possible distribution of distances on the southern route is shown below.  What is the IQR? ![]

[[☃ image 1]]

Since $30$ out of the $32$ data points are contained between $1{,}300$ and $1{,}700$ miles, the middle $50\%$ of values must fall in this $400$ mile range.  So the IQR must be less than or equal to $400$.

On the northern route, both riders rode $2{,}000$ miles, so the median is $2{,}000$. 
 
On the central route $10$ of the $19$ riders rode $3{,}000$ miles.  So, the $10$th highest value must be $3{,}000$.  This is the median.  

On the southern route, the median must be some value between $1{,}300$ and $1{,}700$ miles.  So the median is lowest on the southern route.

The standard deviation measures how far the data are spread out from the mean.  Below is a possible distribution of the riders on the central route.  The data are much more spread out on the central route than on the southern route.


[[☃ image 1]]

All four statements are true:

- The standard deviation for distance traveled on the northern route is very low.
- The interquartile range for distance traveled on the southern route is less than $500$ miles.
- The southern route has the lowest median distance traveled among the three routes.
- The standard deviation on the southern route is less than the standard deviation on the central route.

choices.content: The standard deviation for distance traveled on the northern route is very low.
The interquartile range for distance traveled on the southern route is less than $500$ miles.
The southern route has the lowest median distance traveled among the three routes.
The standard deviation for distance traveled on the southern route is less than the standard deviation for distance traveled on the central route.

assessment_items.question_content:
Samantha, Brooklyn, and Mary went to Bonnaroo music festival where they each attended various concerts.  

- Samantha only went to rock concerts where the sound level was normally distributed between $113$ and $117$ decibels.
- Brooklyn went to $4$ concerts with Samantha and also went to $5$ folk concerts where the sound level was between $95$ and $97$ decibels.
- Mary got sick, so she only attended $2$ shows $-$ one rock concert and one folk concert.

**Which of the following is true? **
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Since Samantha attended only rock concerts, both the median and mean sound levels are between $113$ and $117$ decibels.  

Brooklyn attended more folk concerts than rock concerts, so her median sound value is a folk concert.  Her mean value is between a folk and a rock concert.  

Mary attended $1$ folk concert and $1$ rock concert.  Her median and mean sound values are between a folk and a rock concert.  

Samantha's concerts have the greatest median and mean sound levels.

The standard deviation measure how spread out the data are.   Below are possible distributions for the sound levels of the concerts attended by Samantha, Brooklyn, and Mary, respectively.  


[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]


[[☃ image 3]]
The data for Samantha are the least spread out.  The sound levels for Samantha's concerts have the smallest standard deviation, not the largest.

If Mary had attended an additional rock concert, she would have attended $3$ concerts, and $2$ of them would have been rock.  So, the median sound level would be one of these rock concerts - the median would be greater than it is now.

These three statements are true:

- Among the three girls, Samantha attended the concerts with the greatest mean sound level.
- Among the three girls, Samantha attended the concerts with the greatest median sound level.
- If Mary had attended an additional rock concert, the median sound level of concerts she attended would have been greater than it is now.

choices.content: Among the three girls, Samantha attended the concerts with the greatest mean sound level.
Among the three girls, Samantha attended the concerts with the greatest median sound level.
Among the three girls, Samantha attended the concerts with the greatest standard deviation of sound levels.
If Mary had attended an additional rock concert, the median sound level of concerts she attended would have been greater than it is now.

assessment_items.question_content:
Members of a band class were arguing over which instruments are played by the best academic students.  A survey was conducted, and here are the results:

- All $13$ flute players had a grade point average (GPA) between $3.84$ and $3.88$.  
-  There were only $3$ percussionists.  One had a GPA of $2.4$, one had a GPA of $2.8$, and one had a GPA of $3.2$.
- Among saxophones players, $4$ had a GPA of $3.9$, and the other $5$ had a GPA of $4.0$.


**Which of the following is true? **
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
If one of the flutes played the saxophone, the saxophone GPA distribution would look like this.  The median value would become $3.95$.  The median would decrease by $0.05$.


[[☃ image 1]]

The mean GPA of saxophones, however, would not decrease by very much.  The original mean saxophone GPA is

$\qquad \dfrac{(5\times4.0)+(4\times3.9)}{9} \approx 3.96$  
Suppose the flute player who became a saxophone player had a GPA of $3.84$, the lowest GPA possible.  Then the new mean GPA of saxophones would be 

$\qquad \dfrac{(5\times4.0)+(4\times3.9)+3.84}{10} \approx 3.94$

The mean would only decrease by $0.02$.

All flute GPA's lie within a range of $0.04$, so the IQR is even less than this value.  The $3$ percussionists have GPA's that are $0.4$ apart.  The IQR for percussionists must be much higher than $0.04$.

The standard deviation measures how far the data are spread out from the mean.  If a saxophone player became a percussionist, the data would become much more spread out because the saxophone player would become the new highest value of the data set.

Only this statement is true:

- If one of the flute players played the saxophone instead, the median GPA for saxophones would change by $0.05$.

choices.content: If one of the flute players played the saxophone instead, the median GPA for saxophones would change by $0.05$.
If one of the flute players played the saxophone instead, the mean GPA for saxophones would change by $0.5$.
The interquartile range is greater for flutes than for percussionists.
If one of the saxophone players decided to become a percussionist, the standard deviation for GPA's of percussionists would decrease.

assessment_items.question_content:
In a recent experiment conducted by the Department of Energy, light bulbs were tested to measure how long they lasted.  Each company submitted $20$ light bulbs, which were turned on and left on until they burned out.   Here were the results:

- All $20$ "Super Bright" light bulbs burned out after exactly $1{,}500$ hours.  
- The first "Delightful Bright" light bulb burned out after $1{,}200$ hours, and one additional bulb burned out every $50$ hours until they were all burned out.
- One of the "Pretty Bright" light bulbs burned out after just $16$ hours.  The other $19$ bulbs lasted between $1{,}900$ and $2{,}000$ hours.

**Which of the following is true? **
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Since all the Super Bright bulbs lasted the same length of time, there is absolutely no variability among the data, and the standard deviation for bulb length is $0$ hours.  The other two bulb types have varying data points, so their standard deviations are positive.

The IQR tells a range that contains the middle $50\%$ of a data set.  The plots below show the distributions for Delightful Bright and Pretty Bright, respectively.  

[[☃ image 1]]



[[☃ image 2]]
In order to contain the middle $50\%$ of the data, a greater range is required for Delightful Bright than for Pretty Bright.  The IQR is greater for Delightful Bright.
The data for Delightful Bright is completely flat and symmetrical - it is not skewed either left or right.

Since all the Super Bright light bulbs lasted $1{,}500$ hours, we know the mean is $1{,}500$.  Most of the Pretty Bright bulbs lasted longer than the Super Bright bulbs, but there was one outlier.  Let's calculate the smallest possible mean for the Pretty Bright bulbs.

The smallest mean for the Pretty Bright bulbs would occur if $19$ of the bulbs lasted $1{,}900$ hours:

$\qquad\dfrac{16 + 19\times1900}{20} = 1805.8$ hours

The mean life for Pretty Bright bulbs will always be greater than the mean for Super Bright bulbs.

These three statements are true:

- Super Bright light bulbs have the smallest standard deviation for light bulb life among the three types of bulbs.
- The interquartile range of light bulb life for Delightful Bright is greater than the interquartile range for Pretty Bright.
- Super Bright and Pretty Bright light bulbs cannot possibly have the same mean light bulb life.

choices.content: Super Bright light bulbs have the smallest standard deviation for light bulb life among the three types of bulbs.
The interquartile range of light bulb life for Delightful Bright is greater than the interquartile range for Pretty Bright.
The distribution of Delightful Bright light bulb life is skewed right.
Super Bright and Pretty Bright light bulbs cannot possibly have the same mean light bulb life.

assessment_items.question_content:
At Oakton, Midlothian, and Jefferson high schools, the long jumpers employ different strategies for competition.  

- At Oakton high school, the jumpers are very conservative.  They are always very careful not to fault, which would result in a jump of $0$. But as a result they all jump similar short distances.  
- At Midlothian, the jumpers are very aggressive.  They jump as far as they can, but they risk faulting.  About half the jumpers get a score of $0$ and half jump long distances. (A "long distance" is about twice as far as a "short distance".)
- At Jefferson, the jumpers strike a balance.  They are fairly aggressive, but also careful.  As a result, most jump medium distances (between "long distances" and "short distances"), while one or two of them fault and jump $0$.

**Which of the following is true? **
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Possible distributions of the long jumpers are shown below for the three schools.


[[☃ image 1]]



[[☃ image 2]]


[[☃ image 3]]
The standard deviation measures how spread out the data are from the mean. The data for Midlothian are more spread out than for Oakton.

At Oakton, all the jumps are short distances, so the mean must be a short distance.  At Midlothian, half the jumps are $0$ and half the jumps are long distances.  So the mean is the average of a long distance and $0$:  

$\qquad \dfrac{\text{long distance}+0}{2} = \dfrac{\text{long distance}}{2} = \text{short distance}$

 The means should be about the same.

If $6$ out of the $12$ Midlothian jumpers fault, exactly half the Midlothian jumps will be long and half will be $0$.  So the median jump at Midlothian will be the average of a long jump and $0$ - a short jump.  

Since all the jumps at Oakton are short, the median must be a short value.   The medians will be close at Oakton and Midlothian.
Since most jumps at Jefferson are medium, the median jump will be a medium jump.  Thus, the median at Jefferson is higher than at Oakton.

These three statements are true:

- The mean jump at Oakton is about the same as the mean jump at Midlothian.  
- If $6$ out of the $12$ Midlothian jumpers fault, the median jump at Midlothian is close to the median jump at Oakton.
- The median jump at Jefferson is greater than the median jump at Oakton.

choices.content: The standard deviation for jumpers at Oakton is about the same as the standard deviation for jumpers at Midlothian.
The mean jump at Oakton is about the same as the mean jump at Midlothian.
If $6$ out of the $12$ Midlothian jumpers fault, the median jump at Midlothian is close to the median jump at Oakton.
The median jump at Jefferson is greater than the median jump at Oakton.

assessment_items.question_content:
For his senior project, Richard is researching how much money a college graduate can expect to earn based on their major.  He finds the following interesting facts:

- Basketball superstar Michael Jordan was a geography major at the University of North Carolina.  
- There were only $3$ civil engineering majors from the University of Montana. They all took the exact same job, earning the exact same salary at the same company.
- Of the $35$ finance majors from Wesleyan University, $32$ got high-paying consulting jobs, and the other $3$ were unemployed.

**Which of the following is true? **
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: There are only $3$ civil engineers from the University of Montana and they all earned the same salary, so there is absolutely no variability among these incomes.  So the standard deviation for civil engineers is $\$0$ dollars, which is abnormally low.

All $3$ civil engineering majors earned the same income.  This income is the median, the lowest value, the first quartile, the third quartile, and the highest value of the data set!  Thus the IQR is $\$0$ dollars. So the IQR and standard deviation are equal.

Michael Jordan is one abnormally high outlier for geography majors from UNC.  Outliers can strongly affect the mean, but not the median of a distribution.  The median income for geography majors is not abnormally high.

The mean income for finance majors is pretty high because $32$ out of $35$ got high-paying consulting jobs.  But we know nothing about the IQR for geography majors.  We cannot compare the mean income of Wesleyan finance majors with the IQR of UNC geography majors.

These two statements are true:

- The standard deviation for incomes of civil engineers from the University of Montana is abnormally low.  
- The interquartile range and the standard deviation for civil engineers from the University of Montana are equal.

choices.content: The standard deviation for incomes of civil engineers from the University of Montana is abnormally low.
The interquartile range and the standard deviation for incomes of civil engineers from the University of Montana are equal.
The median income for geography majors from UNC is abnormally high.
There is enough information to compare the mean income for finance majors from Wesleyan with the interquartile range for incomes of geography majors from UNC.

assessment_items.question_content:
In college basketball, some teams get most of their points from just one player, and other teams are more balanced in scoring.  Consider the following facts about the scoring distributions for some NCAA Division I basketball teams. Each team has $12$ players.

- At Baylor University, there are $6$ players who each score $12$ points per game, and there are $6$ players who each score $0$ points per game.
- At the University of Maryland, one player scores $58$ points per game, one player scores $14$ points per game, and the rest of  the players score $0$ points per game.
- At Dartmouth College, $4$ players score $5$ points per game, $4$ players score $6$ points per game, and $4$ players score $7$ points per game.  

**Which of the following is true? **
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The mean number of points per player per game at Baylor is  

$\qquad\dfrac{(6\times12) +(6\times0)}{12}=6$ points per player per game.

The mean number of points per player per game at Maryland is  

$\qquad\dfrac{58 + 14 + (10\times0)}{12}=6$ points per player per game.
  
The mean number of points per player per game at Dartmouth is  

$\qquad\dfrac{(4\times5) +(4\times6)+(4\times7)}{12}=6$ points per player per game.

The means are all the same.

The distribution for points player per per game at Dartmouth looks like this: 
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b74f10e81493b942c554b1535474952b.png)  

and the distribution at Maryland looks like this:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b65b8d61dc3c0ef985bafded541a8058.png)  
The median at Maryland is $0$ and the median at Dartmouth is $6$.

At Maryland, we've discovered that the mean is $6$ points per player per game and the median is $0$ points per player per game - the mean is higher. 
   
At Dartmouth we've found that the median and the mean are both $6$ points per player per game.

These two statements are true:

- The mean number of points per player per game is the same at all $3$ schools.
- At Maryland, the mean number of points per player per game is greater than the median number of points per player per game.

choices.content: The mean number of points per player per game is the same at all $3$ schools.
The median number of points per player per game is the same at all $3$ schools.
At Maryland, the mean number of points per player per game is greater than the median number of points per player per game.
At Dartmouth, the mean number of points per player per game is greater than the median number of points per player per game.

assessment_items.question_content:
Doug is going to ride his bicycle $3{,}000$ miles across the United States, from coast to coast.  He wants to choose the route that will give him the greatest chance of success. Here's what he finds in his research:

-  There have been $2$ attempts on the northern route from Maine to Washington.  Both of those riders made it $2{,}000$ miles and quit in Montana.
- There have been $19$ attempts on the central route from Virginia to California;  $9$ of those riders didn't make it out of Virginia and the other $10$ made it all the way to the Pacific.
- There have been $32$ attempts on the southern route from Florida to San Diego.  One of those riders made it the whole way.  Another realized he was out of shape, and quit before he even started. The other $30$ riders quit somewhere in Texas.  They were evenly distributed between $1{,}300$ and $1{,}700$ miles.


**For the northern route, the median distance covered will be [[☃ dropdown 1]] the mean.**  

**For the central route, the median distance covered will be [[☃ dropdown 2]] the mean.**  

**For the southern route, the median distance covered will likely be [[☃ dropdown 3]] the mean.**
hints: On the northern route, there are only $2$ data points, and they are identical.  This will be the value of both the median and the mean.

On the central route $10$ of the $19$ riders rode $3{,}000$ miles.  So, the $10$th highest value must be $3{,}000$.  This is the median.  

Since not every rider on the central route went $3{,}000$ miles, the mean must be less than the median.
On the southern route, the distribution looks something like this: ![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/75f49806042f0989465172cb0c3d6f2b.png)
The two outliers cancel each other out when calculating both the median and the mean.  Thus the median and the mean should be about equal - somewhere around $1{,}500$ miles.

**The correct answers are: **

- **For the northern route, the median distance covered will be $=$ the mean.**  
- **For the central route, the median distance covered will be > the mean.**  
- **For the southern route, the median distance covered will likely be $=$ the mean.**

assessment_items.question_content:
Samantha, Brooklyn, and Mary went to Bonnaroo music festival where they each attended various concerts.  

- Samantha only went to rock concerts, and the sound level at these concerts was normally distributed between $113$ and $117$ decibels.
- Brooklyn went to $4$ concerts with Samantha and also went to $5$ folk concerts where the sound level was between $95$ and $97$ decibels.
- Mary got sick, so she only attended $2$ shows $-$ one rock concert and one folk concert.

**The median sound level of concerts that Samantha attended was likely [[☃ dropdown 1]] the mean sound level of the concerts she attended.**  

**The median sound level of concerts that Brooklyn attended was [[☃ dropdown 2]] the mean sound level of the concerts she attended.**  

**The median sound level of concerts that Mary attended was [[☃ dropdown 3]] the mean sound level of the concerts she attended.**
hints: In a normal distribution, the median is close to the mean.  We expect the median sound level for Samantha's concerts to equal the mean.

Brooklyn attended $5$ folk concerts and $4$ rock concerts.  A possible distribution of the sound levels of these concerts is shown below.


[[☃ image 1]]
The mean sound level will fall somewhere between "rock" and "folk."  But the median sound level is the fifth-highest value of the data set, which is a folk concert.  The median is less than the mean.
Mary only attended $2$ concerts. Both the median sound and mean sound levels are the half-way between these two data points.

The correct answers are: 

- The median sound level of concerts that Samantha attended was likely $=$ the mean.  
- The median sound level of concerts that Brooklyn attended was < the mean.  
- The median sound level of concerts that Mary attended was $=$ the mean.

assessment_items.question_content:
Members of a band class were arguing over which instruments are played by the best academic students.  A survey was conducted, and here are the results:

- All $13$ flute players had a grade point average (GPA) between $3.84$ and $3.88$.  
-  There were only $3$ percussionists.  One had a GPA of $2.4$, one had a GPA of $2.8$, and one had a GPA of $3.2$.
- Among the saxophone players, $4$ had a GPA of $3.9$, and the other $5$ had a GPA of $4.0$.

**For percussionists, the median GPA is [[☃ dropdown 1]] the mean.**  

**For saxophones, the median GPA is [[☃ dropdown 2]] the mean.**  

**The standard deviation for GPA's of flutes is [[☃ dropdown 3]] the standard deviation for GPA's of saxophones.**
hints:
The median value of percussionists' GPA is $2.8$.  The mean value is  

$\qquad \dfrac{2.4 + 2.8 + 3.2}{3} = 2.8$

The median and mean are equal.
Since $5$ of the $9$ saxophones have a GPA of $4.0$, this is the median, or middle value.  The mean must be less than $4.0$, because there are values of $3.9$ in the data set.

The standard deviation measures how spread out the data are from the mean.  The first plot below shows a possible distribution of GPA's for flutes, and the second plot shows the distribution of GPA's for saxophones.  


[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]
The data are more spread out for saxophones.  The standard deviation is greater for saxophone GPA's than for flute GPA's.

The correct answers are: 

- For percussionists, the median GPA is $=$ the mean.  
- For saxophones, the median GPA is > the mean.  
- The standard deviation for GPA's of flutes is < the standard deviation for GPA's of saxophones.

assessment_items.question_content:
At Oakton, Midlothian, and Jefferson high schools, the long jumpers employ different strategies for competition.  

- At Oakton high school, the jumpers are very conservative.  They are always very careful not to fault, which would result in a jump of $0$. But as a result they all jump similar short distances.  
- At Midlothian, the jumpers are very aggressive.  They jump as far as they can, but they risk faulting.  About half the jumpers get a score of $0$ and half jump long distances. (A "long distance" is about twice as far as a "short distance".)
- At Jefferson, the jumpers strike a balance.  They are fairly aggressive, but also careful.  As a result, most jump medium distances, while one or two of them fault and jump $0$.

**At Oakton, the median jump will likely be [[☃ dropdown 1]] the mean.**  

**Suppose $7$ out of $15$ jumpers faulted at Midlothian.  At Midlothian, the median jump will be [[☃ dropdown 2]] the mean.**  

**At Jefferson, the median jump will likely be [[☃ dropdown 3]] the mean.**
hints: At Oakton, all the jumps will be closely clustered around the same value.  It is likely these jumps will resemble a normal distribution, and there is no reason to believe the median will be be greater or less than the mean.

If $7$ out of $15$ jumpers faulted at Midlothian, slightly more than half of the jumpers will hit long distances.  So the median will be a long distance.  The mean, however, averages the $7$ scores of $0$ and the $8$ long distances.  The mean will be near the middle of a "long" distance and $0$.

At Jefferson, most jumpers hit medium distances, so the median is one of these medium distances.  But there are a couple low outliers of $0$.  These outliers will lower the mean, but not affect the median very much.  The mean is lower than the median.

The correct answers are: 

- At Oakton, the median jump will likely be $=$ the mean.  
- At Midlothian, the median jump will be > the mean.  
- At Jefferson, the median jump will likely be > the mean.

assessment_items.question_content:
For his senior project, Richard is researching how much money a college graduate can expect to earn based on his or her major.  He finds the following interesting facts:

- Basketball superstar Michael Jordan was a geography major at the University of North Carolina.  
- There were only $3$ civil engineering majors from the University of Montana. They all took the exact same job at the same company, earning the same salary.
- Of the $35$ finance majors from Wesleyan University, $32$ got high-paying consulting jobs, and the other $3$ were unemployed.


**For geography majors from UNC, the median income will likely be [[☃ dropdown 1]] the mean.**  

**For civil engineering majors from Montana, the median income will be [[☃ dropdown 2]] the mean.**  

**For finance majors from Wesleyan, the median income will likely be [[☃ dropdown 3]] the mean.**
hints:
An extreme positive outlier like Michael Jordan will increase the mean of a distribution, but it will not strongly affect the median.  It is likely that the mean income of UNC geography majors will be greater than the median.  

(Fun fact: the actual mean income for geography majors from Jordan's $1985$ graduating class was over $\$1$ million.)
There were only $3$ civil engineering majors from Montana, and they all earned the same income.  The mean and median will both be equal to this single value.
The distribution of incomes of finance majors from Wesleyan might look something like this: [[☃ image 1]]

The median is the middle value of the data set.  Since $32$ out of $35$ data points are high incomes, the median must be a high income - it is not affected much by the $3$ incomes of $\$0$.  But the mean will be lowered by each of the $\$0$ incomes.  Thus the median will likely be higher than the mean.

The correct answers are: 

- For geography majors from UNC, the median income will likely be < the mean.  
- For civil engineering majors at Montana, the median income will be $=$ the mean.  
- For finance majors at Wesleyan, the median income will likely be > the mean.

assessment_items.question_content:
In a recent experiment conducted by the Department of Energy, light bulbs were tested to measure how long they lasted.  Each company submitted $20$ light bulbs, which were turned on and left on until they burned out.   Here were the results:

- All $20$ "Super Bright" light bulbs burned out after exactly $1{,}500$ hours.  
- The first "Delightful Bright" light bulb burned out after $1{,}200$ hours, and one additional bulb burned out every $50$ hours until they were all burned out.
- One of the "Pretty Bright" light bulbs burned out after just $16$ hours.  The other $19$ bulbs lasted between $1{,}900$ and $2{,}000$ hours.


**For Super Bright bulbs, the median life will be [[☃ dropdown 1]] the mean.**  

**For Delightful Bright bulbs, the median life will be [[☃ dropdown 2]] the mean.**  

**For Pretty Bright bulbs, the median life will be [[☃ dropdown 3]] the mean.**
hints: Since all the Super Bright bulbs lasted the same amount of time, this value is both the median and the mean.

The Delightful Bright bulbs burned out at consistent intervals of $50$ hours.  The distribution is shown below.  

[[☃ image 1]]

Since the data points are evenly spaced, the mean and median can both be calculated by finding the exact middle of the distribution - between the $10$th and $11$th data point.  The median and mean are both $1{,}675$ hours.
The Pretty Bright bulbs have one very low outlier.  A low outlier will bring down the mean, but it will not affect the median by much.  So the median will likely be greater than the mean.

The correct answers are: 

- For Super Bright bulbs, the median life will be $=$ the mean.  
- For Delightful Bright bulbs, the median life will be $=$ the mean.  
- For Pretty Bright bulbs, the median life will be > the mean.

assessment_items.question_content:
In college basketball, some teams get most of their points from just one player, and other teams are more balanced in scoring.  Consider the following facts about the scoring distributions for some NCAA Division I basketball teams. 
Each team has $12$ players.

- At Baylor University, there are $6$ players who each score $12$ points per game, and there are $6$ players who each score $0$ points per game.
- At the University of Maryland, $1$ player scores $58$ points per game, $1$ player scores $14$ points per game, and the rest of  the players score $0$ points per game.
- At Dartmouth College, $4$ players score $5$ points per game, $4$ players score $6$ points per game, and $4$ players score $7$ points per game.  

**The mean number of points per game for players at Baylor is [[☃ dropdown 1]] the mean number of points per game for players at Dartmouth.**  

**The standard deviation for points per game for players at  Dartmouth is [[☃ dropdown 2]] the standard deviation for points per game for players at Maryland.**  

**The median number of points per game for players at Baylor is [[☃ dropdown 3]] the median number of points per game for players at Maryland.**
hints:
The mean number of points per game at Dartmouth is  

$\qquad\dfrac{(4\times5) +(4\times6)+(4\times7)}{12}=6$ points per game.
  
The mean number of points per game at Baylor is  

$\qquad\dfrac{(6\times 12)+ (6\times0)}{12}=6$ points per game.

The means are the same.

The standard deviation measures how spread out the data are from the mean.  This plot shows the distribution for points per game at Dartmouth.  


[[☃ image 1]]

The distribution of points per game at Maryland looks like this:  
 

[[☃ image 1]]

The data for players at Dartmouth is much less spread out from the mean than the data for players at Maryland.

Here is the distribution for points per game at Baylor:  


[[☃ image 1]]

Since there were $6$ players at Baylor who scored $12$ points per game, and $6$ players who scored $0$ points per game, there is no middle number; the median is half-way between these two values: $6$ points per game.  

At Maryland, there were so many players who scored $0$ points per game that this is the median value.  The median is higher at Baylor.

**The correct answers are: **

- **The mean number of points per game for players at Baylor is $=$ the mean number of points per game for players at Dartmouth.**  
- **The standard deviation for points per game for players at Dartmouth is < the standard deviation for points per game for players at Maryland.**  
- **The median number of points per game for players at Baylor is > the median number of points per game for players at Maryland.**
